beats.text: Most large matrices that show up in practice are not full-rank, they are low-rank, with a few hidden factors explaining almost everything.

visual.sub: A few factors explain the data

beats.text: An m by n matrix can hold up to m times n numbers, but its rank counts how many independent patterns are actually present.

visual.note: U is m by r, V is r by n

beats.text: A rank-r matrix carries only r times m plus n degrees of freedom, so when r is small the saving is enormous.
For a ten thousand square matrix of rank twenty, that is a hundred million numbers collapsing to four hundred thousand.
Real data is rarely exactly low-rank because of noise, but it is very often approximately low-rank, with a sharp drop then a long tail.
Factoring a ten by ten matrix at rank two uses two times twenty, or forty parameters, against one hundred in the dense form.
The singular value decomposition is the factorization that always exists, for every real matrix, and is in a precise sense the best one.
It writes the matrix as a rotation, a non-negative scaling, and another rotation, summing rank-one layers ordered most important first.
Keep only the top k layers and the Eckart-Young theorem says this truncation is the best possible rank-k approximation.

visual.note: leftover error is the discarded energy

beats.text: The singular values are always non-negative, since they are stretch lengths and a length can never be negative.
For singular values six, three, two, one, the energy is the squares, so rank two keeps forty-five over fifty, ninety percent.
Choose k by plotting cumulative energy retained and cutting at the elbow where the curve flattens between signal and noise.
The canonical application, made famous by the Netflix Prize, is collaborative filtering on a sparse ratings matrix that is mostly missing.
Assign every user and every item a latent vector in k dimensions, then predict the rating as a dot product plus biases.
A typical user has rated about a tenth of a percent of the catalog, so the matrix is almost entirely holes.
You cannot run plain SVD here, so you fit only the observed entries by minimizing regularized squared error with gradient descent.

visual.note: the sum skips every missing cell

beats.text: With the example biases and vectors, the dot product is half a point and the prediction comes out to three point nine.
Cold start remains the honest caveat, a new user with no ratings has no factors and the model can only guess the average.
SVD factors mix positive and negative entries, which is optimal but often uninterpretable, since components add and subtract.
Non-negative matrix factorization forces every entry of both factors to be at least zero, so the only way to build data is to add parts.

visual.note: A is m by n, W is m by k, H is k by n

beats.text: Lee and Seung showed that on face images NMF discovers localized features, a nose, an eyebrow, a mouth, because a face is a sum of parts.
NMF is fit by multiplicative updates, element-wise rules that keep factors non-negative automatically and lower the error.

visual.tex: H \leftarrow H \odot \frac{W^{\top}A}{W^{\top}WH}, \quad W \leftarrow W \odot \frac{A H^{\top}}{W H H^{\top}}

beats.text: The error falls steadily as iterations run, converging to a local minimum, so initialization matters and NNDSVD is the common fix.
The honest trade, SVD gives the best fit with orthogonal ordered factors, NMF gives a worse fit but interpretable additive parts.
The secret this chapter shares with Principal Component Analysis is that PCA is just the SVD of the centered data matrix.
Center each column to mean zero, then the principal components are the right singular vectors of the result.
The variance captured by each component is the squared singular value divided by the sample count minus one.
Directions of maximum variance and best low-rank approximation are the same computation, read two different ways.
The same decomposition becomes four tools, compression, principal directions, recommendation, and interpretable additive parts.
The low-rank prior, that a few hidden factors explain most of the data, is one of the most reusable assumptions in machine learning.
